topic: The Bubble Economics That Cause Crashes
title: Why speculative booms eventually turn into painful busts

lesson.learn:
Asset bubbles happen when prices rise far faster than the underlying value can justify. People buy because they expect to sell to someone else at a higher price, which pushes prices higher still. That feedback loop can keep going until confidence breaks, at which point the same crowd rushes for the exit.

Bubble economics matter because they are not random accidents. They are often powered by easy credit, optimism, imitation, and the belief that "this time is different."
lesson.simpler: A bubble is when prices rise because everyone expects them to keep rising. When confidence breaks, the prices can fall very fast.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Bubbles grow from optimism and collapse when buyers stop believing the story.

thirdImageCredit.creator: Mark Bonica from Durham, NH, USA

lesson.deeperDive:
The pattern usually includes a trigger, a flood of money, rising prices, and then a panic. Credit makes it easier to buy. Rising prices make it look as if the investment is smart. But once buyers slow down, the whole structure depends on confidence more than fundamentals.

That is why bubble crashes feel sudden even when the buildup takes years. The real issue is leverage: when people borrow to buy inflated assets, falling prices can force sales and accelerate the drop.
lesson.funFact: A bubble often looks smartest right before it pops.
references: Federal Reserve - asset prices and financial stability

people: Regulators - watch for instability in credit markets
places: Housing markets - where leverage can spread pain
Credit markets - where easy borrowing fuels booms
years: 1630s - tulip mania becomes a classic example
2008 - housing bubble triggers a global crisis
concepts: Speculation - buying because prices are rising
Leverage - using borrowed money to invest
Panic selling - a rush to exit after confidence breaks
Fundamentals - the underlying value of an asset
